justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens (critical finding #1); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended as replacement; timing attack via string equality identified (critical finding #2); crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with correct usage including length check; timestamp design flaw thoroughly documented (critical finding #3, noting validation is impossible in practice); safe_to_deploy is false. Additionally identifies length-extension attacks and input validation issues beyond the rubric requirements.

observations.text: MD5 correctly classified as Critical severity with specific attack vectors (HashClash, hashcat) mentioned
Explicitly distinguishes hash vs HMAC and recommends HMAC-SHA256, noting length-extension vulnerability of naive concatenation
Timing attack identified with practical reproduction steps and correct fix including the length-check guard
Timestamp flaw described exceptionally well — notes that validation will ALWAYS fail, not just sometimes
Production readiness section identifies migration complexity of fixing crypto issues (dual-validation window)
Goes beyond rubric with length-extension attack analysis and partnerId collision risk
No false positives or noise — every finding is legitimate and well-evidenced

Additionally identifies length-extension attacks and input validation issues beyond the rubric requirements.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens (Critical finding #1); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended with clear explanation of why HMAC is the correct primitive over raw hash concatenation; timing attack via string equality identified (Critical finding #3) with crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended; timestamp design flaw noted as an important issue (caller-supplied timestamp, not self-contained); safe_to_deploy is false. Additionally identified the missing expiration enforcement and length-extension attack vulnerability, which go beyond the rubric requirements.

observations.text: MD5 correctly identified as Critical with specific attack vectors (hashclash, brute-force of timestamp space)
HMAC-SHA256 recommended with excellent explanation of why HMAC is structurally superior to hash-with-concatenated-key
Length-extension attack vulnerability called out separately — demonstrates deep cryptographic understanding
Timing attack identified with practical exploitation math (512K requests to reconstruct token)
crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with the important caveat about length check before calling it
Timestamp design flaw noted — caller-supplied timestamp enables brute-force, suggests embedding in token or using JWT
Production readiness section includes migration strategy for rotating existing MD5 tokens
Identified missing expiration enforcement as a separate Critical finding beyond the rubric requirements
Suggested JWT as a standard alternative to hand-rolled token generation

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens (critical_findings[0]); HMAC-SHA256 recommended as replacement with correct rationale about HMAC vs raw hashing (critical_findings[0].fix); timing attack via string equality identified (critical_findings[1]); crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with buffer wrapping detail (critical_findings[1].fix); timestamp storage design flaw noted as important_issues[0] explaining caller cannot know the timestamp and attacker can control it; safe_to_deploy is false.

observations.text: MD5 analysis is thorough — mentions practical collision attacks, HashClash, and the enormous 30-day attack window
Correctly identifies HMAC as the right primitive, not just a stronger hash — explains length-extension attack risk of payload+key concatenation
Timing attack explanation includes academic references (Brumley & Boneh) and practical context about statistical analysis over networks
Identified token expiration is never enforced — a critical finding beyond the rubric minimum
Timestamp design flaw correctly noted: Date.now() used in generation but not returned, and validator accepts caller-supplied timestamp enabling bypass
Suggests JWT as a standard self-contained token format — matches rubric's recommended fix
Production readiness section includes thoughtful migration strategy for existing MD5 tokens
Also caught the length-extension attack vulnerability from secret key concatenation as a separate important issue
